claims.claim: The numeric manifest uses n = 3 over the prime field F_97.
f:{0,1}³→F_97 has the 8 cube values [1,4,2,8,3,5,7,6] in lexicographic corner order (000,001,010,011,100,101,110,111).
claims.evidence: Recipe defines fvals with exactly these 2³ = 8 values; manifest cube_corners/cube_values reproduce them in order.

claims.claim: The multilinear extension f̃ agrees with f on every one of the 8 cube corners.
claims.evidence: Recipe asserts mle(w) == f(w) for all w∈{0,1}³ (manifest agrees_on_cube = true). Independent Sage recompute, building f̃ as an actual polynomial in GF(97)[x0,x1,x2] and evaluating at every corner, also reports AGREE_ON_CUBE: True.

claims.claim: f̃ is multilinear: individual degree ≤ 1 in each variable (here exactly 1 in each), total degree ≤ n = 3.
claims.evidence: Independent recompute: degrees in (x0,x1,x2) are (1,1,1); total degree 3. f̃ has exactly 8 distinct monomials, each a product of a distinct subset of {x0,x1,x2} — confirming the 2ⁿ-dimensional multilinear space.

claims.claim: f̃ is the UNIQUE multilinear polynomial agreeing with f on the cube: 2ⁿ = 8 multilinear coefficients pinned by 2ⁿ = 8 corner constraints, an invertible system.
claims.evidence: The space of multilinear polynomials in 3 variables has dimension 2³ = 8 (one coefficient per subset of variables). The corner-evaluation map is the (invertible, triangular) χ_w basis evaluation, so a multilinear polynomial is determined by its 8 cube values. Sage confirms exactly 8 monomials.
claims.ledger_ref: CHAPTER_BIBLE Ch 7 — 'the multilinear extension (MLE) of a function on the Boolean hypercube {0,1}^n ... the unique multilinear agreeing on the cube.'

claims.claim: An off-cube evaluation: f̃(2,5,3) = 5 over F_97, computed via MLE(x)=Σ_w f(w)·∏ᵢ(xᵢwᵢ+(1−xᵢ)(1−wᵢ)).
claims.evidence: Recipe: basis-sum formula gives offcube_mle = 5 at (2,5,3). Independent recompute via the explicit polynomial P(2,5,3) over GF(97) also yields 5. The point (2,5,3) is off the cube (coordinates outside {0,1}), so this is a genuine extension value.

claims.claim: The figure shows the n = 2 picture: the unit square's 4 corner values [1,4,3,6] extended to the smooth bilinear surface, which agrees on the corners, is linear along each axis, has edge-midpoint value 2.0 and center value 3.5.
claims.evidence: Recipe bilinear bil(a,b): bil(0.5,0)=2.0 = ½·1+½·3 (average of corners (0,0)=1 and (1,0)=3, the straight-line restriction along x1); bil(0.5,0.5)=3.5 = mean(1,4,3,6). The manim scene recomputes bil in pure Python and its SCENE_VALUES match the manifest with no drift.

scene_value_check.note: Empty list = no drift; every value the manim scene displays (4 corner values, center 3.5, edge midpoint 2.0, agrees-on-cube true) matches the Sage manifest's picture_* fields.

ledger_refs: CHAPTER_BIBLE Ch 7 'Fingerprints: Why Polynomials Catch Liars' — Core god-node (C47): 'Multilinear extension (MLE)'; content walk: 'the multilinear extension (MLE) of a function on the Boolean hypercube {0,1}^n, and an argued case for why multilinear, not univariate — n variables of degree 1 each, so 2^n evaluation points compress to a degree-n object the verifier can handle one variable at a time.'
CHAPTER_BIBLE Ch 8 — 'the MLE is the object summed' (the form in which claims about a table become claims about a polynomial; the input sum-check consumes).

graphify subgraph (community 30): Jolt --assumes--> Multilinear extension (MLE); MLE --assumes--> MLE-structured table (evaluable in O(log N) time, so no party commits to it); path MLE→Sumcheck via SuperSpartan (2 hops).

adversarial_findings.finding: Risk that 'the' MLE is presented as unique without justification — a reader could object that many polynomials pass through 8 points.
adversarial_findings.resolution: Uniqueness is established two independent ways and stated explicitly in prose: (a) dimension count — the multilinear space in 3 variables is 2³ = 8-dimensional and the 8 corner constraints are an invertible (triangular χ_w) system; (b) Sage recompute shows f̃ has exactly 8 monomials. The non-uniqueness objection is named in stuck_points and answered (the freedom is removed by the multilinear restriction, not by the point count).

adversarial_findings.finding: Possible confusion that 'multilinear' means total degree 1 (a flat plane).
adversarial_findings.resolution: Recompute shows total degree 3 with individual degree 1 per variable; prose and the animation make the distinction concrete — the n=2 surface bends (a saddle) but is straight along each axis, demonstrated by sliding a point along an edge. Both numbers (individual degree 1, total degree n=3) are stated separately.

adversarial_findings.finding: Off-by-one / arithmetic risk in the off-cube value MLE(2,5,3).
adversarial_findings.resolution: Re-derived two independent ways over F_97: the basis-sum formula in the recipe (5) and an explicit polynomial P(2,5,3) built in GF(97)[x0,x1,x2] (5). Both agree; no arithmetic error.

adversarial_findings.finding: Risk that the figure asset is empty or stale, or that the scene values drift from the manifest.
adversarial_findings.resolution: Re-ran the Sage recipe via run_sage.py (validate_figure passed); assets/figures/multilinear-extension.svg is 109,780 bytes and copied into figures/. validate_scene_values returns [] (no drift) for all scene values.
